title: EdgeRelations

lean_statement.name: Adjacent
lean_statement.code:
/-- `vв‚Ѓ` and `vв‚‚` are *adjacent in `G`*: there is some edge of any
orientation between them. Definitionally `sus G vв‚Ѓ vв‚‚` from
def_3_2; the readable name exists so downstream prose statements
such as claim 3.1's "no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent" can be cited
without unfolding to `sus`. -/
def Adjacent (G : CDMG О±) (vв‚Ѓ vв‚‚ : О±) : Prop := sus G vв‚Ѓ vв‚‚

lean_statement.name: EdgeInto
lean_statement.code:
/-- `EdgeInto G vв‚Ѓ vв‚‚` -- the edge between `vв‚Ѓ` and `vв‚‚` is *into
`vв‚Ѓ`*, i.e. has an arrowhead at `vв‚Ѓ` (either a directed edge
`vв‚Ѓ вџµ[G] vв‚‚` or a bidirected edge `vв‚Ѓ вџ·[G] vв‚‚`). Definitionally
`hus G vв‚Ѓ vв‚‚` from def_3_2. -/
def EdgeInto (G : CDMG О±) (vв‚Ѓ vв‚‚ : О±) : Prop := hus G vв‚Ѓ vв‚‚

lean_statement.name: EdgeOutOf
lean_statement.code:
/-- `EdgeOutOf G vв‚Ѓ vв‚‚` -- the edge between `vв‚Ѓ` and `vв‚‚` is *out
of `vв‚Ѓ`*: a directed edge `vв‚Ѓ вџ¶[G] vв‚‚`. Definitionally
`tuh G vв‚Ѓ vв‚‚` from def_3_2. Note the deliberate asymmetry with
`EdgeInto`: bidirected edges are excluded here, because a
bidirected edge has arrowheads at both endpoints and so is "into"
both vertices but "out of" neither. -/
def EdgeOutOf (G : CDMG О±) (vв‚Ѓ vв‚‚ : О±) : Prop := tuh G vв‚Ѓ vв‚‚

lean_explanation:
The file equips def 3.2's primitive edge relations with three readable prose names matching the LN's English phrasings. Throughout, `О± : Type*` is the ambient vertex type, `G : CDMG О±` is the graph under discussion, and `vв‚Ѓ vв‚‚ : О±` are arbitrary vertices.

Each of `Adjacent`, `EdgeInto`, and `EdgeOutOf` is a `Prop`-valued one-line `def` that aliases one of def 3.2's underlying relations. `Adjacent G vв‚Ѓ vв‚‚` (LN: "$v_1$ and $v_2$ adjacent in $G$") unfolds to `sus G vв‚Ѓ vв‚‚`, the "any edge of any orientation between $v_1$ and $v_2$" relation. `EdgeInto G vв‚Ѓ vв‚‚` (LN: "edge into $v_1$") unfolds to `hus G vв‚Ѓ vв‚‚`, which holds when there is an arrowhead at $v_1$ вЂ” either a directed edge $v_1 \hut v_2$ or a bidirected edge $v_1 \huh v_2$. The LN's companion phrasing "edge into $v_2$" is the *same* predicate with the arguments swapped: `EdgeInto G vв‚‚ vв‚Ѓ`. `EdgeOutOf G vв‚Ѓ vв‚‚` (LN: "edge out of $v_1$") unfolds to `tuh G vв‚Ѓ vв‚‚`, which holds exactly when there is a directed edge $v_1 \tuh v_2$ вЂ” equivalently $(v_1, v_2) \in G.E$ вЂ” and deliberately excludes bidirected edges.

The file also ships a `@[simp]` characterisation lemma for each of the three names (`adjacent_iff`, `edgeInto_iff`, `edgeOutOf_iff`), each just `Iff.rfl`, so callers can rewrite freely between the prose name and its def 3.2 underlying form. A small companion lemma `edgeOutOf_iff_hut` records that the LN's two equivalent spellings of "out of $v_1$" вЂ” $v_1 \tuh v_2$ and $v_2 \hut v_1$ вЂ” collapse to the same Lean proposition by definition. Finally, `Adjacent.symm` proves the (LN-implicit) symmetry of adjacency by case-splitting on the three possible edge orientations and invoking `G.L_symm` from def 3.1 on the bidirected branch.
design_choices:
**A `def`, not an `abbrev` or new notation.** The goal of this row is to introduce *readable Lean identifiers* for prose-level use, not new graph structure. An `abbrev` would dissolve into `sus` / `hus` / `tuh` on sight, defeating the purpose: downstream statements such as claim 3.1's "no two nodes in $J$ are adjacent" would render in goals and error messages as the def 3.2 primitive rather than as the LN's English name. A `notation` token would pile yet another arrow on top of the six already introduced in def 3.2, when what we want at this layer is precisely the English. A `def` is the right unit: it survives intact in goal output, it can be unfolded explicitly via the `@[simp]` `_iff` lemmas when a proof needs the underlying relation, and it keeps the def 3.2 arrows as the formal-syntax layer with these new identifiers as the prose-syntax layer on top.

**Convention: arrowhead at the *first* argument.** Item 2 of the LN lists two sentences вЂ” "into $v_1$" and "into $v_2$" вЂ” that share the same predicate, differing only in which vertex argument the arrowhead lands on. Rather than introducing two predicates, we follow the LN's natural wording and let `EdgeInto G vв‚Ѓ vв‚‚` mean "into $v_1$", with the arrowhead at the first listed vertex; the LN's "into $v_2$" sentence becomes `EdgeInto G vв‚‚ vв‚Ѓ`. This convention also matches the underlying def 3.2 relation `hus`, which already picks out the first vertex as the arrowhead site, so no swapping is needed when unfolding.

**Bidirected edges deliberately excluded from `EdgeOutOf`.** A bidirected edge $v_1 \huh v_2$ has arrowheads at *both* endpoints, so it is "into" both vertices but "out of" neither. The LN encodes this asymmetry by listing $\huh$ only on the "into" side of item 2 and never on the "out of" side of item 3. This is load-bearing downstream: the directed-walk and ancestor relations introduced in later rows all condition on *directed* edges only, never bidirected ones, and baking that asymmetry into the prose names now keeps those later definitions clean.

**The LN's two spellings of "out of" collapse to one Lean proposition.** Item 3 offers both $v_1 \tuh v_2$ and $v_2 \hut v_1$ as ways to express "out of $v_1$". In Lean these are literally the same proposition вЂ” both unfold to $(v_1, v_2) \in G.E$ вЂ” so we pick `tuh G vв‚Ѓ vв‚‚` as the canonical form and record the alternative spelling as a separate `Iff.rfl` lemma `edgeOutOf_iff_hut`, giving readers who arrive via the $\hut$ side an immediate bridge to the canonical form.
